filer: Pennsylvania Office of Consumer AdvocateDelaware Division of the Public AdvocateIllinois Office of Attorney General ,
source_text: sources/comments/files/20251205-5270__Pennsylvania-Office-of-Consumer-AdvocateDelaware-D/Joint Consumer Advocates (PA, DE, IL) Reply Comments - Large Loads ANOPR RM26-4.txt
overall_summary: Three state consumer advocate offices (PA, DE, IL) urge FERC to adopt a cooperative federalism model that preserves state jurisdiction while setting federal minimum standards for large load interconnection, curtailment, and cost allocation. They argue that residential ratepayers must be shielded from cost shifts caused by data center load growth, and that new large loads should bear 100% of the network upgrade costs their interconnections require without crediting mechanisms. They also support the 20 MW threshold, aggregate-demand-based thresholds, gross metering for hybrid facilities, and expedited but enforceable curtailment commitments.

quotes.text: Residential ratepayers should not suffer unreliable electricity service or pay unreasonably high rates for inferior, unreliable grid service as a result of the interconnection of large loads that do not offset their demand through the addition of timely, local, and adequate capacity resources.
quotes.concern: Residential consumers face rate and reliability harm from large loads that lack adequate capacity resources

quotes.text: FERC should adopt a cooperative federalism model to govern large load interconnections, curtailments, and transmission cost allocations that preserves existing state jurisdiction.
quotes.concern: FERC should adopt cooperative federalism that does not displace state authority

quotes.text: curtailments should protect current customers, especially residential consumers, over new large load customers that cause the resource inadequacy
quotes.concern: Curtailment priority should favor existing residential customers over new large loads that caused the shortfall

quotes.text: FERC can and should recognize that RTOs must depart from pro-rata transmission curtailment in the rare circumstances where (1) the large loads have caused the resource inadequacy; and (2) the RTO has (a) exhausted all of its options for avoiding transmission curtailment of customers, and (b) has established that those transmission customers that would be disproportionately affected by the curtailment – new large loads – have options to reduce their need for transmission capacity, including alternative supply options, compensation for demand response, or reduced transmission rates for the interruptible service they are provided.
quotes.concern: RTOs should depart from pro-rata curtailment when large loads caused the shortage and have alternative supply options

quotes.text: treating new large loads differently from other load is reasonable and necessary to ensure reliable service and just and reasonable rates for existing consumers, especially residential consumers
quotes.concern: Differential treatment of large loads is justified by their unique reliability and rate impacts

quotes.text: FERC can and must appropriately treat new large loads attributable to data centers differently from other kinds of load because: (1) data centers are creating unique difficulties for the grid and impacting a uniquely vulnerable market at a uniquely expensive level; and (2) investments in data centers can be speculative and uncertain in nature, increasing the risk of a severe market correction that would create stranded costs.
quotes.concern: Data center loads warrant distinct treatment due to grid stress and speculative investment risk

quotes.text: FERC should stay the course and adopt the proposed 20 MW threshold to define large loads and hybrid facilities because this MW threshold helps protect against reliability concerns from large loads or hybrid facilities by requiring proper interconnection studies that will reduce reliability risks for all electricity consumers.
quotes.concern: The 20 MW threshold should be retained to ensure proper interconnection study coverage

quotes.text: the threshold 'should apply to the customer's aggregate power requirement rather than to a single point of interconnection.'
quotes.concern: Threshold should be applied to aggregate demand to prevent evasion through multiple connection points

quotes.text: The definition of large load and hybrid facilities should use the planned full operational capacity, even if the facility is planning to ramp up its operations over time. Otherwise, a new large load or hybrid facility could circumvent the threshold requirement by phasing in its operations.
quotes.concern: Threshold should be based on planned full capacity to prevent phased-in evasion

quotes.text: FERC should approach injection and withdrawal rights according to grid-protective assumptions that prevent free-riding and regulatory evasion.
quotes.concern: Hybrid facility injection and withdrawal rights must use gross-load assumptions to prevent cost-shifting

quotes.text: the Joint Consumer Advocates recommend that hybrid facilities have their load and generation components metered separately, to ensure fair and accurate cost allocation of transmission costs.
quotes.concern: Separate metering of load and generation is needed for hybrid facilities to ensure accurate cost allocation

quotes.text: any curtailable or dispatchable commitments made by large loads and hybrid facilities must be verifiable, enforceable, and subject to penalties for non-performance. There should not be limits on the number of curtailable events or the number of times for curtailments as some request.
quotes.concern: Curtailment commitments must be fully enforceable with no caps on curtailment events

quotes.text: large load and hybrid facilities should be responsible for 100% of the network upgrade assigned to them through the interconnection studies and that these costs should not be offset through a crediting mechanism.
quotes.concern: Large loads must pay 100% of assigned network upgrade costs with no crediting mechanism

quotes.text: FERC should revisit a 'but-for' test when determining which costs should be paid 100% by the large load customers, similar to the test traditionally used by FERC for cost allocation for the interconnection of generators.
quotes.concern: A but-for causation test should determine which upgrade costs large loads must pay in full

quotes.text: Without cost allocation reform, existing customers will face mounting transmission costs due to the cumulative effect of a patchwork of individually negotiated interconnection agreements, reviewed under a Mobile-Sierra standard, which comprise a significant portion of current grid usage.
quotes.concern: Current Mobile-Sierra review of individual agreements fails to protect existing customers from cumulative cost shifts

bins.name: Preserve State Jurisdictional Authority
bins.description: The filers support a cooperative federalism model that sets federal minimum standards while fully preserving state authority over retail load interconnections and curtailments. They argue FERC should clarify that RTOs may depart from pro-rata curtailment when large loads caused the shortage, but only after exhausting alternatives and only when the affected customers have other supply options.

bins.name: Retain 20 MW Threshold, Aggregate Basis
bins.description: The filers support the proposed 20 MW threshold and oppose raising it, arguing a lower threshold protects against reliability risks and deters evasion. They also argue the threshold should apply to a customer's aggregate demand and to planned full operational capacity rather than initial or per-point capacity.

bins.name: Gross Metering for Hybrid Facilities
bins.description: The filers support grid-protective injection and withdrawal assumptions for hybrid facilities and recommend separate gross metering of load and generation components. Net metering, they argue, allows hybrid customers to evade their fair share of transmission costs and shifts those costs to other ratepayers.

bins.name: Enforce Curtailment Commitments Fully
bins.description: The filers support expedited interconnection for facilities that commit to curtailment or dispatch, but insist those commitments must be verifiable, enforceable, and subject to penalties. They oppose any caps on the number of curtailment events, arguing such caps shift risk onto other customers who did not receive the expedited treatment.

bins.name: 100% Cost Assignment, No Credits
bins.description: The filers urge FERC to assign 100% of network upgrade costs to the large load or hybrid facility that caused them, using a but-for causation test or a higher-of policy, with no crediting mechanism. They argue the current Mobile-Sierra review of individually negotiated agreements leaves existing customers exposed to mounting cumulative transmission cost increases.

bins.name: Sound Interconnection Studies Required
bins.description: The filers support comprehensive and technically sound interconnection studies for large loads and hybrid facilities. They argue the 20 MW threshold and aggregate demand basis ensure the study process captures all facilities that pose reliability risks, and that joint studies of hybrid facilities must not compromise study quality.

bins.name: Protect Residential Ratepayers from Cost Shifts
bins.description: Cost protection for residential ratepayers is the central theme of the filing. The filers oppose any cost socialization or crediting that moves large-load network upgrade costs onto existing customers, and argue data centers' speculative investment nature makes stranded cost risk especially acute.

bins.name: Gross Load Basis for Co-located Generation
bins.description: The filers recommend that hybrid facilities with co-located generation be metered and studied on a gross load basis, not net, to prevent free-riding on the transmission system and subsidization by other customers.

bins.name: Curtailment Tied to Causation, Fully Enforceable
bins.description: The filers support flexible or curtailable interconnection options but insist curtailment must be tied to causal responsibility: loads that caused resource inadequacy should bear curtailment risk first. Any flexibility commitment must be enforceable without event-count caps.

bins.name: Speculative Data Center Investment Risk
bins.description: The filers flag that investment in new data center loads is more speculative than typical load growth, citing risk of a market correction that would strand network upgrade costs. This risk reinforces their argument that large loads should pay up front and that FERC should not build crediting mechanisms that defer cost recovery.

bins.name: Mobile-Sierra Review Leaves Cost Gap
bins.description: The filers argue that individually negotiated transmission security agreements reviewed under the Mobile-Sierra standard provide insufficient consumer protection against the cumulative cost impact of many large load additions. They urge FERC to establish minimum cost allocation standards that address this structural gap.
